section.title: Exchange of Multilingual Translation Memory Data
section.para: An XLIFF document is bilingual: it declares a single srcLang and a single trgLang. A translation memory, however, is often required to hold or exchange content across more than two languages. TMX addresses this within a single file, because a <tu> can hold any number of <tuv> elements, one per language.

This document recommends addressing the same requirement not by making an individual XLIFF document multilingual, which would be a departure from the XLIFF Core data model, but by packaging multiple bilingual XLIFF files together, one per source/target language pair, along with a manifest that identifies the relationship among them. This keeps every individual file fully conformant with, and processable by, any existing XLIFF 2.x tool, while still allowing a translation memory covering many languages to be exchanged as a single unit.

A multilingual translation memory is rarely built from a single source language: it typically accumulates from projects extracted from whatever source language each originating document happened to use. Accordingly, the files in a package are not required to share a common srcLang. A single package MAY combine bilingual files representing any number of independent source/target language pairs, for example an en-es.xlf file alongside an unrelated fr-de.xlf file, with the manifest simply recording the srcLang and trgLang that each file declares.

section.title: Package Structure
section.para: A translation memory exchange package is a ZIP archive (as defined in [ZIP APPNOTE]) with the following content:

listitem.para: One or more bilingual XLIFF files, each conforming to [XLIFF-2.3-part2] or later, each independently declaring its own srcLang and trgLang. Files in the same package are not required to share a common srcLang.

A single manifest file, described in Manifest File, that groups the XLIFF files in the package into one or more aligned sets by shared source language and records the language pair each file represents.

section.para: The XLIFF files in a package are not required to sit at the root of the archive. They MAY instead be organized into any folder structure the package author finds convenient, for example one folder per language pair. The manifest's href value for each file gives that file's actual path within the package, so a consumer locates every file through the manifest rather than by assuming a particular layout.

Every file listed in the manifest belongs to exactly one aligned set: a group of files that share a common srcLang and were extracted from the same source content. A file with no counterpart sharing its source is simply the only member of its own aligned set. Corresponding translation units across the files in an aligned set MUST use the same <unit> id value (and, where segmented, the same <segment> id value). This is what lets a consumer treat aligned-set membership as a guarantee rather than a hint: a match against a given <unit> id in one file of the set is known to correspond to the same source context in every other file of the set, which lets a tool synthesize a multi-target match across every language in the set directly from a single-language lookup, without re-querying each file separately. This mirrors, at the package level, the alignment that a multilingual <tu> provides in a single TMX file, while extending it to packages whose source language differs from one aligned set to the next.

A tool that does not support this manifest format is not prevented from using the package. Because every file it lists remains an independent, fully conformant XLIFF document, such a tool MAY simply extract the archive, ignore xliff-manifest.xml, and ingest each bilingual XLIFF file on its own, exactly as it would ingest any other XLIFF file it was given individually. The manifest adds cross-file structure for tools that understand it; it is not required to make the package's contents usable.

The same fallback applies if a ZIP archive of bilingual XLIFF files has no xliff-manifest.xml at all: such an archive does not conform to this document as a Translation Memory Exchange Package, but a tool MAY still treat it as an unlinked collection of independent bilingual memories and ingest each file individually.

section.title: Manifest File
section.para: The manifest is an XML file, named xliff-manifest.xml, placed at the root of the package. Its root <tmPackage> element MAY carry a packageId attribute identifying the package and a created attribute recording when it was assembled; both are informational and carry no processing requirement. It groups the package's XLIFF files into one or more <alignedSet> elements, each declaring the shared srcLang of its members and MAY carry a <description> child element with a human-readable summary of what the set represents. Within an aligned set, each file is listed as a <file> element giving the href path to the file within the package and the trgLang it represents, and MAY carry a hash attribute so a consumer can verify the file was not corrupted or altered in transit.
section.programlisting: <tmPackage xmlns="urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:tm-manifest:1.0" version="1.0" packageId="pkg-2026-finance-tm" created="2026-07-10T13:43:00Z"> <alignedSet id="set-01" srcLang="en"> <description>Financial terminology memory extracted from Q2 2026 projects.</description> <file href="en-es.xlf" trgLang="es" hash="sha256:3451e18e0aa54c65398509e1decd963e1ea1fafa4dee37fde6b1e2585c0f74ec"/> <file href="en-fr.xlf" trgLang="fr" hash="sha256:ec076145e71a8c08b9e78253ceac02bc9a0f98a56577216987ab6dea15e7b457"/> </alignedSet> <alignedSet id="set-02" srcLang="fr"> <file href="fr-de.xlf" trgLang="de" hash="sha256:6dc9abf3aace245aefb08f4220e8355dc237de9ef289c60c87abd734ed552986"/> </alignedSet> </tmPackage>
section.para: A manifest is not required to declare the same srcLang for every aligned set it contains, as the example above illustrates: en-es.xlf and en-fr.xlf both originate from English source content and are grouped together in set-01, while fr-de.xlf is an unrelated bilingual file with French as its source language and forms the single-member set-02 on its own.

The hash attribute lets an exchanging tool make two decisions before it opens or parses a potentially large XLIFF file. When synchronizing against a package it has seen before, a tool can compare a file's hash value in the new manifest against the hash of the copy it already holds, and skip reparsing entirely when they match, rather than reparsing every file on every sync. After extracting a file from the archive, a tool can also recompute its hash and compare it against the value recorded in the manifest to detect truncation or corruption introduced during transport, before that file's content is allowed to reach a TM store.

A hash value is expressed as algorithm:digest, where algorithm identifies the hash function used (sha256 is RECOMMENDED) and digest is the lowercase hexadecimal digest it produces. Where a <file> entry carries a hash value, an exchanging tool SHOULD validate the extracted file's content against it before committing that content to a TM store, and SHOULD treat a mismatch as a sign of a corrupted or incomplete transfer rather than importing it. A matching hash only guards against accidental corruption in transport: because both a file and its manifest entry are typically produced by the same party, a hash alone does not protect against deliberate tampering by anyone able to modify the package.

para.emphasis: The manifest namespace, element and attribute names, and XML Schema shown above are provisional and subject to review by the OASIS XLIFF TC before this Note advances beyond Committee Note Draft.

section.title: Example
section.para: A translation memory accumulated from an English-source project translated into Spanish and French, together with an unrelated project whose source was French and whose target was German, would be packaged as:

Each of en-es.xlf, en-fr.xlf, and fr-de.xlf is an ordinary bilingual XLIFF document as described in XLIFF as a Translation Memory Format, and each can be consumed independently by a tool that only needs one language pair, or together by a tool that reads xliff-manifest.xml to reconstruct the full set. en-es.xlf and en-fr.xlf share a common source language and belong to the same aligned set, so they MUST be aligned by <unit> and <segment> id as described in Package Structure; fr-de.xlf has no source language in common with the other two files and forms its own aligned set, outside that alignment.
As described in Package Structure, the same three files MAY instead be organized into one folder per language pair:
section.programlisting: tm-package.zip ├── xliff-manifest.xml ├── en-es/ │ └── en-es.xlf ├── en-fr/ │ └── en-fr.xlf └── fr-de/ └── fr-de.xlf
section.para: with the manifest updated to match:
section.programlisting: <tmPackage xmlns="urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:tm-manifest:1.0" version="1.0" packageId="pkg-2026-finance-tm" created="2026-07-10T13:43:00Z"> <alignedSet id="set-01" srcLang="en"> <file href="en-es/en-es.xlf" trgLang="es" hash="sha256:3451e18e0aa54c65398509e1decd963e1ea1fafa4dee37fde6b1e2585c0f74ec"/> <file href="en-fr/en-fr.xlf" trgLang="fr" hash="sha256:ec076145e71a8c08b9e78253ceac02bc9a0f98a56577216987ab6dea15e7b457"/> </alignedSet> <alignedSet id="set-02" srcLang="fr"> <file href="fr-de/fr-de.xlf" trgLang="de" hash="sha256:6dc9abf3aace245aefb08f4220e8355dc237de9ef289c60c87abd734ed552986"/> </alignedSet> </tmPackage>
section.para: Both layouts are valid and equivalent: a conformant consumer resolves every file through the href value recorded in the manifest rather than by assuming either layout.
